mappings.rationaleAI: Education systems shaped by political agendas address formation of educational policy (A.1) and types of systems (A.3). Digital world pedagogy and contemporary challenges address education in the 20th century (B.8).

Higher education from medieval origins addresses development of universities in the 12th-century renaissance (B.3.c). Contemporary challenges including digital learning and tuition fees address education in the 20th century (B.8).
Ancient Greek civilization covering the polis and political concepts provides context for ancient Greek education (B.1.d).
The Roman Empire covering imperial administration and Romanization of cultures provides context for ancient Roman education (B.1.e).
Confucianism covering civil service examinations and state ideology addresses ancient Chinese education (B.1.b) and Chinese education from 618 to 1911 (B.4.b).
European history from 500 to 1500 CE covering kingship, political authority, and cultural development provides context for education in the European Middle Ages (B.3).
The Byzantine Empire covering Greek intellectual tradition and Christian faith addresses Byzantine education and Greek Christian and humanistic culture (B.2.b).
Islamic history covering the mosque as community center and caliphate as governance system provides context for Islamic education (B.2.d).
The Renaissance covering humanism and classical revival addresses development of Renaissance education (B.5) and the humanistic tradition in Italy (B.5.b).

The Protestant Reformation covering church-state relations, literacy through vernacular Bible translation, and educational impact addresses education during the Reformation and Counter-Reformation (B.5.d).
The Catholic Reformation covering missionary activity and global expansion addresses education during the Counter-Reformation (B.5.d).

The Enlightenment covering intellectual achievements and the creation of a public sphere for critical discussion addresses European education in the 17th and 18th centuries (B.6) and educational theories and practices (B.6.b).
Rousseau's educational theory, critique of civilization, and theories of natural human goodness directly address educational theories and practices of the 17th and 18th centuries (B.6.b).
Humanism as a worldview grounded in science and reason connects to the development of Renaissance education through Arabic and secular influences on humanism (B.5.a).
Modern China covering economic transformation and cultural identity provides context for the modernization of education in Asia (B.8.c).
Contemporary India covering economic and social transformation provides context for the modernization of education in Asia (B.8.c).
Modern Japan from the Meiji Restoration covering modernization provides context for the modernization of education in Asia (B.8.c).
Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states addresses education in colonies and newly emerging nations (B.8.d).
African history from prehistoric origins through independence movements addresses modernization of education in Africa (B.8.c) and education in newly emerging nations (B.8.d).

The Industrial Revolution covering social transformation in Britain addresses Western education in the 19th century (B.7) and the social setting of nationalism, industrialism, and urbanization (B.7.a).
Socialism as a political ideology covering social democracy and communist approaches provides context for political, social, and economic trends in 20th-century education (B.8.a).
Roman Catholicism covering early Christianity and medieval papacy addresses Christian education and development of monastic schools (B.3.a).
Puritanism covering education and its transplantation to colonial New England addresses education during the Reformation (B.5.d) and European influences in New World educational development (B.6.c).
Colonial America covering trans-Atlantic flows of ideas and cultural mixing addresses European influences in New World educational development (B.6.c).
Globalization covering interconnected economic, political, and cultural processes addresses political, social, and economic trends in 20th-century education (B.8.a).
Psychological science of learning through conditioning theories addresses traditional and experimental educational movements in the West (B.8.b).

Global historical survey of childhood covering reform movements and international organizations' impact on children addresses Western education in the 19th century (B.7) and education in the 20th century (B.8).
Philosophy in Islamic lands covering intellectual traditions provides context for Islamic education (B.2.d).
Gandhi's philosophical positions on citizenship and education connect to the spread of Western educational practices to Asian countries (B.7.d) and modernization of education in Asia (B.8.c).

Encompasses the study of ancient Greek and Roman civilizations including philosophy, drama, and political thought, directly relevant to ancient Greek education (Plato, Aristotle) and ancient Roman education systems.

Covers Buddhism originating in South Asia circa 5th century BC with monastic institutions and doctrinal traditions, relevant to ancient Indian education where Buddhist monastic centers like Nalanda were major educational institutions.

Covers the evolution from elite oral reading to mass literacy driven by printing, paper-making, and public education, including the eighteenth-century literacy boom, directly relevant to Western education development in the 19th and 20th centuries and the relationship between literacy and economic development.

Covers Calvinism's influence on education, the arts, and social institutions across multiple continents, relevant to education during the Reformation and Counter-Reformation and educational theories in the 17th and 18th centuries.
